notes:
The game runs perfectly but I had an issue where it froze during loading after the intro video. After checking the logs it appears the problem was cause by the limit of files you can open set by my OS. Running `ulimit -Hn` or `ulimit -Sn` will give the respective hard and soft limit of allowed open files. You can change this in the `/etc/security/limits.conf` like shown here:
"https://easyengine.io/tutorials/linux/increase-open-files-limit"

I think this solution is universal to all linux distros. There are other ways to do this like trough some systemd commands if are using it.

My defaults were something like 4k and 2k I now switched them to 100000 and 150000 an the game works perfectly ( You need to reboot after changing the file ).

I initially set the limits to 20000 and 25000 and the game worked but crashed during the tutorial. So set it at least to 100000.

Not all distros will be affected by this.
Looking at some forums Fedora might have this problem as well.

notes:
Steam overlay showed 60 FPS, but I had severe stuttering. I fixed it by editing bot h/etc/systemd/system.conf and /etc/systemd/user.conf, uncommenting the line containing DefaultLimitNOFILE= and adding the parameter 524288. Like this:
DefaultLimitNOFILE=524288
After that it ran silky smooth, similar to the Windows version.

notes: I previously rated this as Borked, turns out I'm just dumb. Try to run steam in the terminal and see if there's any Vulkan initialisation error. If so, try to run vulkaninfo (you may have to install vulkan-utils) and restarting Steam. I thought it was working fine before because I tried other games and they worked fine, but more modern games didn't really work, so I ended up looking at this issue more closely. Game runs perfectly fine with no tweaks.
title: Sekiro™: Shadows Die Twice

notes:
I reported "Borked" earlier due to the game hanging after the first cinematic. It seems this was due to ESYNC and a "too many open files" error I saw when I ran steam through a terminal. I was able to successfully run the game initially by running Steam with PROTON_NO_ESYNC=1. 

I eventually increased the number of open files my Linux user account could have to an absurd amount, and this made it so I could run the game with Esync turned on. The info on how to do this can be found on Lutris' Esync how-to wiki article.
 
I've played for about an hour at this point with no real complaints beyond the fact it always shows Xbox buttons even though I have a PS4 controller. I assume this is an issue with the game since that is the behavior even when I force disable Steaminput, and I don't have "PS4 Configuration Support" enabled under the general controller settings.

notes:
At first, I had some problems running from a non-ext4 formatted parition (NTFS), after which I reinstalled the game on my home partition (ext4). Then, perhaps because I had little space on that partition, the game automatically removed some of its files, which made the complete installation ~3GB, while Steam reported that the donwload would be ~12GB. After a third installation, everything seems to work fine!
I am using a Playstation 3 Dualshock 3 controller, connected with Bluetooth. If I connect my controller before starting the game, and disable Steam's Playstation configuration support (under Settings -> Controller -> General Controller Settings) it works well.

title: Sekiro™: Shadows Die Twice

notes: For those who enable nvidia-drm modesetting to eliminate screen tearing on NVIDIA Optimus systems, VSYNC must be disabled or it will create ugly hangs.  However, Sekiro does not allow VSYNC to be turned off in its options.  You must disable "sync to V-blank" in the NVIDIA Settings app, and even then there are some occasional small hangs.  The game is perfectly playable in the exception of these intermittent hangs.
title: Sekiro™: Shadows Die Twice

notes:
Optimus graphics with Proton produce ugly hangs (2-3 seconds if not unrecoverable) unless VSYNC is disabled.  You cannot disable VSYNC in the game's settings or with command line parameters, so ESYNC must be disabled to avoid the ugly hangs.  The ugly hangs are replaced by stuttering, but it is at least playable.

I would like to see a feature in newer Proton versions to force off VSYNC in Vulkan for Optimus systems.
title: Sekiro™: Shadows Die Twice

notes:
I've played through the game one and a half time so far, and it's been great. The listed system have no issues running it at 4K@60fps, but I bought that thing to weather the performance penalty.

I also played the game on a lower end system for a few days. I can't recall the specs, but it's a laptop from 2014 with an i7 and a GTX 770M. It ran the game at a stable 1080p@20-30fps on lowest settings.

There is a rare issue where there's a crash on hitting the continue option on the main menu, but restarting the game has worked 100% of the time. Changing the workspace can also cause the game to crash, but that is also rare.

All in all, if you played Dark Souls 3 with Proton and was happy with its performance, Sekiro will likely give you a more stable experience.

notes:
After playing for a while started to notice unbearable FPS drops. This is due to Esync. So don't follow the naive approach by slapping PROTON_NO_ESYNC and instead just google "How to ESYNC" and adjust ulimits. After that game runs as smooth as in Windows. 

DO: 
- Google "How to Esync" and follow the guide
DO NOT:
- don't add PROTON_NO_ESYNC=1 to launch options.
OPTIONAL:
- GameMode from Feral Interactive. Doesn't do much (or anything at all), but if it helps you sleep at night use it.
- On KDE disabling the compositor (Allow applications to block compositing option) actually has no effect after Esync fix.

title: Sekiro™: Shadows Die Twice

notes:
This game runs great, other than the frame cap. I haven't played it long, but I spent a fair amount of time disabling esync and fiddling with other options before i figured out what the stuttering/freezing problem was. Turns out that if your system does not use systemd  you might have a low limit on the number of files your system can open. On gentoo (openrc) it is set to 4096 by default. So I followed the guide on the lutris esync wiki and set it to 524288, which allows for esync to work on my system, removing the stutter and freezing without adding any launch options. 

This is the guide:
https://github.com/lutris/lutris/wiki/How-to:-Esync

I ignored all the systemd related stuff and  edited /etc/security/limits.conf.  it worked like a charm.
title: Sekiro™: Shadows Die Twice

notes: Using the launch option `PROTON_NO_FSYNC=1 %command%` I am able to get 60FPS with max graphics
title: Sekiro™: Shadows Die Twice

notes: Crashes as soon as it renders anything 3D (After first cutscene, or when loading any existing save). This is fixed by disabling Esync, but performance remains pretty terrible. I get 30-50 fps all the time, regardless of if I'm on Low or Max settings, and no matter which area I'm in. Tried other tweaks but none seemed to fix performance. Technically playable, but experience is subpar compared even to a console.
